questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions
questions.question: In the given figure (a parallelogram), \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \vec{a}\), \(\overrightarrow{AD} = \vec{b}\). Which expression represents \(\overrightarrow{AC}\)?
questions.hint: Parallelogram law: diagonal from common tail equals sum of adjacent sides.
questions.explanation: In parallelogram \(ABCD\) with \(\overrightarrow{AB}=\vec{a}\) and \(\overrightarrow{AD}=\vec{b}\), the diagonal \(\overrightarrow{AC} = \vec{a} + \vec{b}\) by the parallelogram law. Option A is correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

explanations_tiered.definition: In a parallelogram, the diagonal from the common tail equals the sum of the two adjacent side vectors.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Parallelogram law: \(\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \overrightarrow{AC}\) when \(\vec{a}\) and \(\vec{b}\) are adjacent sides from A.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two arrows from A: one to B (a), one to D (b). The diagonal from A to C is the sum: a + b.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In vector addition, \(\vec{a}+\vec{b}\) completes the parallelogram, giving diagonal AC.
worked_solution: Step 2: Parallelogram law states sum is diagonal from A
reasoning_steps: Recall parallelogram law of vector addition.
Identify the two adjacent side vectors.
Their sum is the diagonal from the common initial point.
misconceptions: Some think diagonal is difference (a - b) or b - a.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Parallelogram: AC = AB + AD = a + b.

glossary_entry.definition: The diagonal from the common vertex equals the sum of the adjacent side vectors.

flashcard.front: In parallelogram ABCD, AB = a, AD = b. What is AC?
flashcard.back: a + b. Parallelogram law: diagonal = sum of adjacent sides.

visual_system.visual_description: Parallelogram ABCD with A at origin, B to the right, D up, C at B + D. Diagonal AC shown.
visual_system.caption: Parallelogram ABCD with diagonal AC
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Parallelogram with vertices A(0,0), B(3,0), D(1,2), C(4,2). Vector AB horizontal right, AD diagonal up right, AC diagonal from A to C.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: In parallelogram, AB = a, AD = b. AC = ?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: a + b. Parallelogram law: diagonal = sum of sides.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sum of adjacent side vectors equals the diagonal.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw parallelogram to visualise vector addition.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Express diagonal vectors as sum of sides
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Using the grid squares (each 1 unit), vector \(\vec{a}\) goes from (1,1) to (4,5). What is \( |\vec{a}| \)?
questions.explanation: \(\Delta x = 4-1=3\), \(\Delta y = 5-1=4\). Magnitude = \(\sqrt{3^2+4^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25} = 5\) units. Option A gives \(\Delta x\); B gives \(\Delta y\); D is \(\sqrt{3^2+4^2-?}\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnitude is the distance between initial and terminal points using the distance formula.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Count grid squares to find Δx and Δy, then apply Pythagoras.
explanations_tiered.beginner: From (1,1) to (4,5): right 3, up 4. Hypotenuse = 5 (3-4-5 triangle).
misconceptions: Using Δx or Δy alone instead of vector sum.

glossary_entry.definition: Count squares to get components, then Pythagoras.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use grid to show right triangle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of a vector quantity?

options.C: A quantity with both magnitude and direction
options.D: A quantity with neither magnitude nor direction

questions.hint: Vectors require both size and orientation.
questions.explanation: Vectors are fully described by magnitude (numerical value) and direction. Option A defines scalars; option C is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors include force, velocity, displacement; scalars have magnitude only.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A vector needs a number and an arrow direction. Like '10 km north'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Magnitude gives size, direction gives orientation. Both are required for a vector.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector quantities are elements of a vector space, possessing both norm and direction.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of vector from PDF.
Identify option that includes both magnitude and direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vector = magnitude + direction. Scalar = magnitude only.

glossary_entry.definition: A quantity fully described by both a magnitude and a direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A quantity with both magnitude and direction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. A vector has both magnitude and direction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A vector has magnitude 10 and points due east. What are its components?
questions.hint: East is positive x-direction, no vertical component.
questions.explanation: East corresponds to positive x-axis, so components are (10, 0). Option A is correct; B is north; C is west; D is south.

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector pointing east has positive x-component and zero y-component.
explanations_tiered.beginner: East means go right. No up or down. So (10, 0).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Due east is along positive x-axis, so components are (10, 0).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Unit vector east is \(\vec{i}\), so vector = \(10\vec{i} = \binom{10}{0}\).
misconceptions: Placing magnitude in y-component (north) instead of x.

glossary_entry.definition: A vector pointing east has components (|v|, 0).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce mapping of cardinal directions to axes.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Compare a scalar and a vector. Which statement is true?

options.B: Scalars have direction; vectors have magnitude only
options.C: Vectors have direction; scalars do not

questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction; scalars have magnitude only. Option C correctly states this difference.

explanations_tiered.definition: Scalars have magnitude only; vectors have magnitude AND direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Direction is the distinguishing feature: vectors have it, scalars do not.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Scalars: just a number (like 10 kg). Vectors: number + direction (like 10 km north).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vectors require direction for complete description; scalars do not.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalars are rank-0 tensors; vectors are rank-1, possessing directional information.
misconceptions: Thinking scalars also have direction, or vectors lack direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vector = has direction; scalar = no direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors have direction; scalars do not.

flashcard.front: How do vectors differ from scalars?
flashcard.back: Vectors have direction; scalars have magnitude only.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Vectors have direction; scalars have magnitude only.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors include directional information; scalars do not.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Vector = magnitude + direction. Scalar = magnitude only.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Vectors have direction; scalars do not.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Addition and subtraction of column vectors
questions.question: If \(\vec{p} = \binom{-5}{2}\) and \(\vec{q} = \binom{3}{-4}\), find \(\vec{p} + \vec{q}\).

source_trace.structural_element: Addition and subtraction of column vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Add column vectors by adding corresponding components.
misconceptions: Adding x to y or incorrect sign handling.

glossary_entry.definition: Add top numbers, add bottom numbers.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Watch for sign errors with negative numbers.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The position vector \(\overrightarrow{OP} = 4\vec{i} - 3\vec{j}\). What are the coordinates of point \(P\)?
questions.hint: \(\vec{i}\) coefficient is x-coordinate, \(\vec{j}\) coefficient is y-coordinate.
questions.explanation: Position vector \(x\vec{i} + y\vec{j}\) corresponds to point \((x,y)\). Here \(4\vec{i} - 3\vec{j}\) gives \((4,-3)\). Option C is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: position vector xi + yj corresponds to point (x,y)

explanations_tiered.definition: In \(\vec{r} = x\vec{i} + y\vec{j}\), the terminal point is \((x,y)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The coefficients of \(\vec{i}\) and \(\vec{j}\) give the x and y coordinates directly.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Standard basis representation: \((4,-3)\) is the coordinate vector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Coefficients of i and j give point coordinates.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find point from position vector in i,j form
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Coefficient of i = 4 (x), coefficient of j = -3 (y) → (4,-3).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions
questions.question: In rectangle \(ABCD\), \(E\) is midpoint of \(AB\), \(F\) is midpoint of \(BC\). If \(\overrightarrow{AB} = 2\vec{a}\) and \(\overrightarrow{BC} = 2\vec{b}\), what is \(\overrightarrow{EF}\)?
questions.explanation: \(E\) midpoint of \(AB\) so \(\overrightarrow{EB} = \vec{a}\). \(F\) midpoint of \(BC\) so \(\overrightarrow{BF} = \vec{b}\). Then \(\overrightarrow{EF} = \vec{a} + \vec{b}\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

explanations_tiered.definition: The vector from midpoint E of AB to midpoint F of BC equals sum of half-side vectors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: E is halfway from A to B, so EB = a. F is halfway from B to C, so BF = b. EF = EB + BF = a + b.
reasoning_steps: Determine vector from E to B.
Determine vector from B to F.
misconceptions: Using AE instead of EB, or wrong order of subtraction.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector between midpoints equals sum of half-side vectors.

flashcard.front: In rectangle, AB=2a, BC=2b, E midpoint AB, F midpoint BC. EF = ?
flashcard.back: a + b. EB = a, BF = b, so EF = a + b.

visual_system.visual_description: Rectangle ABCD with A bottom-left, B bottom-right, C top-right, D top-left. E midpoint of AB, F midpoint of BC. Vector EF shown.
visual_system.caption: Rectangle ABCD with midpoints E and F
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Rectangle with vertices A(0,0), B(4,0), C(4,2), D(0,2). E at (2,0), F at (4,1). Vector EF from (2,0) to (4,1).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: EF in rectangle with midpoints E and F?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: a + b. EF = EB + BF = a + b.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector between midpoints = sum of half-side vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show path E→B→F to derive sum.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

source_trace.structural_element: Addition and subtraction of column vectors

misconceptions: Subtracting in reverse order (v - u) or mishandling double negative.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A vector has components \(x = -5\sqrt{3}\) and \(y = 5\). What is its direction angle \(\theta\) from the positive \(x\)-axis?
questions.hint: \(\tan \theta = y/x = 5/(-5\sqrt{3}) = -1/\sqrt{3}\). x negative, y positive → quadrant II.
questions.explanation: \(\tan \theta = \frac{5}{-5\sqrt{3}} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\). Reference angle = \(30^\circ\). Quadrant II (x<0, y>0) gives \(\theta = 180^\circ - 30^\circ = 150^\circ\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction angle \(\theta\) requires quadrant adjustment when components are negative.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Compute reference angle from \(|y/x|\), then adjust based on signs of x and y.
explanations_tiered.beginner: x negative (left), y positive (up) → Northwest (Quadrant II). Reference 30°, so 180°-30°=150°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: arctan(y/x) returns -30°, but since x<0,y>0, add 180°: \(\theta = 150^\circ\).
worked_solution: Step 3: x negative, y positive → Quadrant II
misconceptions: Using arctan without quadrant adjustment gives -30° (or 330°).

glossary_entry.definition: Adding 180° or 360° to arctan(y/x) based on component signs to get correct angle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle measured from positive x-axis, adjusted for quadrant.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find direction angle with negative components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Quadrant II: ref=30°, θ=180°-30°=150°.
